Part of : Java Multithreading Mastery in 15 Days

5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

To read this article for free, click here
Let’s Start With A Question
By now, you have written code like this many times.
synchronized(lock){
// Critical Section
}We know what it does. Only one thread enters. Others wait. Simple.
But… Have you ever wondered:
What exactly happens when execution reaches the word synchronized?
Does Java create a lock?
Does it call the operating system?
Where is this lock stored? Let’s open the hood.
Think About A Hotel Room
Imagine a hotel. Every room has a key. Whoever holds the key can enter the room. Everyone else waits.
The interesting part is this: The hotel does not create a new key every time someone arrives.
The key already belongs to the room. Java works in a very similar way.
Every Java Object Has A Monitor
